ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general steamed fish class; no specific Cantonese black-bean-sauce variant exists. Closest defensible match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers steamed fish broadly; no black-bean-sauce-specific code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept for steamed fish with black bean sauce identified in the international release.

main_ingredients: whole fish (sea bass, snapper, or grouper)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant or home serving of steamed fish with black bean sauce is approximately 300 g, representing a portion of a whole steamed fish with sauce for one person at a shared meal.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Food Composition Database — Steamed Fish (Cantonese preparation); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175159 (Cooked sea bass)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for steamed fish used as the primary anchor for core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, sugar). Micronutrient values (potassium, magnesium, phosphorus, selenium, vitamins) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked sea bass (FDC ID 175159) and adjusted for sauce contribution. Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (1.8 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.6 g/100 g), and cholesterol (45.0 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA FDC sea bass data with adjustment for added vegetable oil drizzle. No single approved-source entry exists for the exact dish 'steamed fish with black bean sauce'; adaptation is documented.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index — not applicable; food contains negligible carbohydrates. Reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed fish with black bean sauce contains only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Per the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed GI literature (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), high-protein low-carbohydrate fish dishes have negligible glycaemic impact and GI is conventionally classified as not applicable. Glycemic load per 300 g serving is effectively 0 given the negligible carbohydrate content. glycemic_index set to null and gi_category set to 'not_applicable' per Project Veritas v2 critical rule for low/zero-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy sauce and fermented black beans (350 mg sodium per 100 g; approximately 1050 mg per 300 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium soy sauce and limit sauce consumption, as a full 300 g serving provides approximately 1050 mg sodium, representing 46% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended by the American Heart Association.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (1.2 g per 100 g) with high lean protein (16.5 g per 100 g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Steamed fish with black bean sauce is an excellent choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its negligible carbohydrate load and high-quality protein, which promotes satiety without causing postprandial blood glucose spikes; however, added sugar in the sauce should be minimised.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Rich in omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids from fish (1.6 g PUFA per 100 g) and low in saturated fat (1.2 g per 100 g), with beneficial selenium (32.5 mcg per 100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Steamed fish is cardioprotective due to its omega-3 fatty acid content, which reduces triglycerides and inflammation; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose this preparation over fried fish and monitor sodium intake from the soy-based sauce.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids from fish and ginger
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The omega-3 fatty acids in fish and the anti-inflammatory compounds in ginger may help reduce joint inflammation; use high-quality oils for the finishing drizzle and avoid excessive refined vegetable oils.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Fish with Black Bean Sauce (豆豉蒸魚, Dòuchǐ Zhēng Yú) is a classic Cantonese dish in which a whole fresh fish — typically sea bass (鱸魚), snapper, or grouper — is steamed over high heat for 8–12 minutes until just cooked through. The fish is seasoned with julienned ginger, scallions, and fermented black beans (douchi), then finished with a drizzle of sizzling hot vegetable oil poured over fresh scallion shreds, which aromatically wilts the garnish and releases fragrant compounds. A mixture of soy sauce, a small amount of sugar, and sometimes fish stock is poured over the fish before or after steaming. The dish has a delicate, clean flavour with savoury umami depth from the fermented black beans and soy sauce, and a tender, moist, flaky texture. It is a staple at Cantonese family dinners, celebratory banquets, and Chinese New Year feasts, symbolising abundance and prosperity. Nutritionally, it is high in lean protein (16.5 g/100 g), moderate in fat (5.2 g/100 g, predominantly unsaturated), very low in carbohydrates (1.2 g/100 g), and rich in vitamin B12 (2.2 mcg/100 g), selenium (32.5 mcg/100 g), niacin (4.5 mg/100 g), and vitamin D (4.5 mcg/100 g). The sodium content is elevated (350 mg/100 g) due to soy sauce and fermented black beans, making sodium management important for individuals with hypertension. It is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management and cardiovascular health due to its low glycaemic impact and omega-3 fatty acid content.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and primary micronutrients are anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo data for steamed fish preparations and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (cooked sea bass, FDC ID 175159). Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and cholesterol are estimated from USDA FDC data for cooked sea bass with sauce adjustment. No single approved-source entry exists for the exact dish 'steamed fish with black bean sauce'; the closest HPB and USDA entries were used with documented adaptation. Water content is set to -1 as moisture data for this specific preparation is not reported in the consulted sources.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture data for steamed fish with black bean sauce as a composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central entries consulted (FDC ID 175159). Saturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.8 g/100 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.6 g/100 g), and cholesterol_mg (45.0 mg/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for cooked sea bass (FDC ID 175159) adjusted for sauce and oil contribution; these are not directly reported in the HPB source. trans_fat_g set to 0.0 as no trans fats are expected in this traditionally prepared steamed fish dish. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (1.2 g/100 g); gi_category set to 'not_applicable' and glycemic_index set to null per Project Veritas v2 critical rule. GI source references University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care) as the peer-reviewed authority for GI classification of low-carbohydrate fish dishes. The dish name in the input record ('Steamed fish with black bean sauce') and the description reference 'Qing Zheng Yu' (plain steamed fish with soy sauce); both variants are captured in aliases as they share a closely related nutritional profile. The dietary tag 'plant_based' in the original input has been removed as this is a fish-based dish. Duplicate portion entry in the input has been deduplicated to a single 300 g serving. The original input cited Hong Kong Polytechnic University as a source; this has been replaced with approved sources (HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central) per Project Veritas v2 source policy.
